title: The Other Cheek
subtitle: On the mountain, the Lord overturns the law of vengeance and commands a more terrible love

stillness: Be still, wretched sinner, and let the noise of your wounded pride fall silent.
One thing only is asked of your heart: that it be ready.
"My heart is ready, O God, my heart is ready."
gospel: You have heard that it was said: An eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth.
But I say to you: do not resist evil.
If anyone strikes you on your right cheek,
turn to him the other also.
And from him who takes away your cloak,
do not withhold even your tunic.
To him who would go to law with you and take your tunic,
let go to him your mantle as well.
behold: [Attend to each thing, as if you were present.]
Behold the Lord Jesus upon the mountain, overturning the old law of vengeance.
Among the Jews the command stood: an eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth —
a measure set to restrain the cruelty both of those who wound and those who are wounded.
But behold a greater justice, whose mercy is exalted above all judgment.
See the One who, struck, did not strike back;
who, when He was cursed, did not curse in return.
Behold Him upon the cross: He let His garments be stripped from Him,
and divided among the soldiers — and He did not contend.
Look now upon the four steps that climb toward peace:
to render no more; to render less; to render no evil at all;
and at the summit, to stand ready to bear yet more.
consider: Consider: to will another's punishment is no good in itself.
It is good only for the order of justice, or the sinner's correction, or that others, seeing him punished, be held back from evil.
Where no such good is hoped for, but only scandal feared, you are bound to lay vengeance down.
The evil of guilt must always be resisted; but the evil of injury, never out of lust for revenge.
In the Law there was retribution; in the Gospel, grace — which teaches that virtue is kept even through the loss of our own limbs.
Chrysostom says: If you tear out another's eye, you have not recovered your own — you have lost your patience along with the limb.
The devil procures wounds of the body for the sake of wounds of the soul.
If you do not strike back, you seem conquered by a man — but you have conquered the devil.
Turn the other cheek: the cheek of the inner man, so the inner self be not moved when the outer is struck.
Augustine: Not only do not strike back, but if he wishes to strike the other, bear it patiently.
Chrysostom again: He who strikes back fulfils the law — but not of Christ.
You say: he deserves to be struck back. Granted — but you are not worthy to strike, for you are the disciple of Him who, reviled, reviled not again.
Bede: What is so great as to offer the cheek to the striker? The fury of indignation is broken, anger is stilled, and through your patience he is changed, or drawn to repentance.
By fulfilling this command, man is conformed to Christ, the devil is conquered, and full peace is won among men.
And while you long to avenge the garment of your body, you lose the most precious garment of your soul.
Such meditation in tolerance leads, in the end, to the passion of martyrdom.
Blessed, then, is he who in his very heart stands ready for all things.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, most gentle Master of all humility and patience,
grant to me, the least and simplest of Your servants,
that, setting myself low and beneath all others,
I may long to be despised by all, and trodden underfoot,
and that, bearing injury patiently in body or in goods,
I may keep a heart made ready to endure still more,
and may help, as far as I am able, all who ask of me in their bodily need.
Grant also that I may be able not only to love my friends,
but even my enemies — those who persecute me in heart, in word, or in deed —
to do them good, and to bless them, and to pray for them,
that by Your grace I may be counted worthy among Your sons and chosen ones. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Dum enim vis vindicare vestem corporis, pretiosissimum animæ vestimentum perdis.
latinJewel.english: For while you long to avenge the garment of your body, you lose the most precious garment of your soul.
